pathophysiology_summary: Vulvovaginal candidiasis is Candida (usually C. albicans) overgrowth when the vaginal flora is disturbed (here recent broad-spectrum antibiotics; also pregnancy, diabetes, oestrogen), producing intense vulval itch/burning, a thick white 'cottage-cheese' non-malodorous discharge, external dysuria and superficial dyspareunia with vulvovaginal erythema. It is benign and treated with an antifungal. The ground-truth task is to distinguish it from bacterial vaginosis (thin grey, fishy odour), trichomoniasis/other STIs (frothy green-yellow discharge, 'strawberry cervix', partner/STI risk) and to screen for pelvic inflammatory disease / upper-tract infection (fever, deep pelvic pain, abnormal bleeding), and to confirm pregnancy status because it changes antifungal choice (avoid oral fluconazole in pregnancy — use topical clotrimazole). Recurrent disease (>=4 episodes/year) warrants looking for an underlying cause (e.g. diabetes) and a longer regimen.

differential.evidence_basis: Itch/burn + thick white non-malodorous discharge after antibiotics.
Thin grey fishy-smelling discharge — argues against (no odour here).
Frothy green-yellow discharge / STI-risk features — screen; would need different treatment/partner management.
Screen fever, deep pelvic pain, abnormal bleeding — needs urgent treatment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Candidiasis may lead, but BV/trichomonas/STI and PID must be screened, and pregnancy status confirmed (drug choice), before treatment.

differential.evidence_basis: Itch/burn + thick white non-malodorous discharge, no PID/STI features, post-antibiotic — vulvovaginal candidiasis; confirm pregnancy status for drug choice.
No frothy malodorous discharge or STI-risk features; escalation/testing if they emerge.
No fever/deep pelvic pain/abnormal bleeding; escalation target if they develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative STI/PID screen, candidiasis leads; antifungal + irritant avoidance, with a pregnancy check (drug choice), an STI/PID safety-net and recurrence consideration.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; settles with an antifungal; investigate if recurrent (>=4/year).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is vulvovaginal candidiasis (telehealth-appropriate treatment).

red_flags.description: PID / STI / alternative diagnosis — fever, deep pelvic or lower-abdominal pain, abnormal/intermenstrual/postcoital bleeding, or an offensive/frothy discharge or STI-risk features
red_flags.clinical_significance: PID needs urgent treatment; an STI needs testing/partner management and different treatment.

red_flags.description: Pregnancy (changes antifungal choice — avoid oral fluconazole, use topical) or recurrent candidiasis (>=4 episodes/year — investigate underlying cause e.g. diabetes)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Affects safe drug choice and prompts a work-up for recurrence.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unsafe antifungal in pregnancy / missed underlying driver.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing all discharge to thrush without screening odour, STI-risk and pelvic-pain/systemic features.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Not checking pregnancy for drug choice
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Oral fluconazole is avoided in pregnancy — use topical; confirm pregnancy status before choosing.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Speculum/pelvic examination and vaginal pH / wet mount / whiff test
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Antifungal (oral or topical) + irritant avoidance (confirm pregnancy for drug choice); urgent review for PID; STI testing if risk features; investigate recurrence.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing PID/STI, or an unsafe antifungal choice in pregnancy.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Treat vulvovaginal candidiasis with a topical imidazole (e.g. clotrimazole) or a single oral fluconazole dose (avoid oral fluconazole in pregnancy — use topical). Avoid vulval irritants. Consider BV/trichomonas/STI and PID as alternatives; investigate recurrent candidiasis (e.g. diabetes).
